Claude Code 在 Windows 上登录失败并出现 OAuth 超时

Claude Code login fails with OAuth timeout on Windows

github.com 作者 sh1mmer 分数 218 评论 293 2026/04/07 23:04
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 飞行前检查表
  2. 我已经搜索了现有问题，但尚未报告这是一个错误报告（请为不同的错误提交单独的报告）我正在使用最新版本的克劳德代码
  3. 怎么了？ Claude Code 无法在 Windows 上登录，并且总是失败并出现以下错误： OAuth 错误：超出 15000 毫秒超时 我已尝试使用我的 Google 帐户多次登录，但每次登录都会失败，并出现相同的 OAuth 超时错误。这完全阻止了我使用克劳德代码。应该发生什么？ Claude Code 应该成功完成 Google 登录流程并让我登录应用程序而不会超时。错误消息/日志 OAuth 错误：超过 15000 毫秒超时 重现步骤
  4. 在 Windows 上打开 Claude Code 开始使用 Google 登录 在浏览器中完成登录流程 返回 Claude Code Claude Code 显示：OAuth 错误：超出 15000ms 超时 多次重试登录 每次都会发生相同的错误
  5. 克劳德模型 不确定/多个模型 这是回归吗？我不知道最后工作版本 2.1.92 Claude 代码版本 2.1.92 平台 Anthropic API 操作系统 Windows 终端/Shell WSL（适用于 Linux 的 Windows 子系统） 其他信息 无响应

Original Summary

  1. Preflight Checklist
  2. I have searched existing issues and this hasn't been reported yet This is a single bug report (please file separate reports for different bugs) I am using the latest version of Claude Code
  3. What's Wrong? Claude Code cannot log in on Windows and always fails with this error: OAuth error: timeout of 15000ms exceeded I have tried logging in multiple times with my Google account, but the login fails every time with the same OAuth timeout error. This completely blocks me from using Claude Code. What Should Happen? Claude Code should complete the Google login flow successfully and sign me into the app without timing out. Error Messages/Logs OAuth error: timeout of 15000ms exceeded Steps to Reproduce
  4. Open Claude Code on Windows Start login using Google Complete the sign-in flow in the browser Return to Claude Code Claude Code shows: OAuth error: timeout of 15000ms exceeded Retry login multiple times The same error happens every time
  5. Claude Model Not sure / Multiple models Is this a regression? I don't know Last Working Version 2.1.92 Claude Code Version 2.1.92 Platform Anthropic API Operating System Windows Terminal/Shell WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) Additional Information No response

评论（默认中文）

xantronix 2026/04/07 23:46
正如黑客新闻上的人们抱怨生产力和创造力套件的订阅模式一样，订阅开发工具（实际上是服务）的张开双臂拥抱寻求卸载行为本身让我想知道如何以及为什么这么多人渴望直接投入。我明白了。 法学硕士是很酷的技术。

这是否是十年前大量一次性 JavaScript 框架背后的同一现象的症状？ 是同辈压力、害怕错过机会吗？ 从根本上来说，我怀疑是这样；当然，我认为最高管理层很少强制要求使用特定的语言或框架，而法学硕士代表了前所未有的权力杠杆，从商业角度来看，它可以更大程度地发挥先发优势。 （是的，我知道本地模特对许多人来说已经变得多么“足够好”。）

关于处理容量问题的能力的回拨，我在这里确实没有任何有用或可操作的说法。 是否有任何迹象表明商店或个人贡献者已经制定了应急计划，以实物形式减少 LLM 的使用，以减轻这些未知因素？ 我知道潜在的（并且经常实现的）收益远远超过全力以赴的风险，但是，在更宏伟的时间和环境计划中，长期承诺开始变得更加明显风险。 我有意避免在这里“回避问题”；如果不是法学硕士，而是其他工具或服务，那么对这些事件的反应会更加务实，在与不稳定的供应商打交道时，不会那么犹豫地投入时间在内部解决方案上。

As much as people on Hacker News complain about subscription models for productivity and creativity suites, the open arms embrace of subscription development tools (services, really) which seek to offload the very act itself makes me wonder how and why so many people are eager to dive right in. I get it. LLMs are cool technology.

Is this a symptom of the same phenomenon behind the deluge of disposable JavaScript frameworks of just ten years ago? Is it peer pressure, fear of missing out? At its root, I suspect so; of course I would imagine it's rare for the C-suite to have ever mandated the usage of a specific language or framework, and LLMs represent an unprecedented lever of power to have an even bigger shot at first mover's advantage, from a business perspective. (Yes, I am aware of how "good enough" local models have become for many.)

I don't really have anything useful nor actionable to say here regarding this dialling back of capability to deal with capacity issues. Are there any indications of shops or individual contributors with contingency plans on the table for dialling back LLM usage in kind to mitigate these unknowns? I know the calculus is such that potential (and frequently realised) gains heavily outweigh the risks of going all in, but, in the grander scheme of time and circumstance, long term commitments are starting to be more apparently risky. I am purposefully trying to avoid "begging the question" here; if instead of LLMs, this were some other tool or service, reactions to these events would have been far more pragmatic, with less of a reticence to invest time on in-house solutions when dealing with flaky vendors.

rurp 2026/04/07 23:54
HN 是一个大型社区，其中既有重视新颖性的人，也有重视简单性和可靠性的人。除非您认出完全相同的名字并采取这些相互矛盾的观点，否则大多数情况下可能是不同的人群。

过去几年里，似乎每个法学硕士主题都充满了帖子，称最新的热门人工智能工具/方法使他们的工作效率高得令人难以置信，而其他人则表示，他们发现同样的事情令人印象深刻。

HN is a big community that has always had a mix of people who value newness as a feature vs those who prioritize simplicity and reliability. Unless you're recognizing the exact same names taking these contradictory opinions it's probably different groups of people for the most part.

It seems like every LLM thread for the past couple years is full of posts saying that the latest hot AI tool/approach has made them unbelievably more productive, followed by others saying they found that same thing underwhelming.

echelon 2026/04/08 00:05
> 我明白了。法学硕士是很酷的技术。

我认为你们中的很多人都没有真正尝试过克劳德代码，或者也许您的理解是错误的。

我完成了 10 倍的工作。我以前所未有的速度和速度在堆栈的所有层进行操作。

在有人指责我是“氛围编码员”之前，我已经构建了五个 9 主动-主动资金轨道，每天以 50kqps+ 的速度转移数十亿美元，以及许多其他重磅平台工程工作。认真的高级工程师十多年。

这不仅仅是一项“酷技术”。我们已经退出打孔卡阶段。而这很难或不可能恢复。

如果您没有看到这些相同的成功，我有理由认为您使用了错误的方法。

老实说，我不喜欢订阅服务、超大规模的权力集中，或者我无法在本地运行 Opus。但这并不重要——该工具以它的形状存在，我必须以它呈现的方式使用它。我希望有一种更加民主和开放的不同产品，但目前市场还没有提供这一点。

这就好像您接入了光纤或宽带，却被要求返回 ISDN/拨号。

> I get it. LLMs are cool technology.

I don't think many of you have legitimately tried Claude Code, or maybe you're holding it wrong.

I'm getting 10x the work done. I'm operating at all layers of the stack with a speed and rapidity I've never had before.

And before anyone accuses me of being some "vibe coder", I've built five nines active-active money rails that move billions of dollars a day at 50kqps+, amongst lots of other hard hitting platform engineering work. Serious senior engineering for over a decade.

This isn't just a "cool technology". We've exited the punch card phase. And that is hard or impossible to come back from.

If you're not seeing these same successes, I legitimately think you're using it wrong.

I honestly don't like subscription services, hyperscaler concentration of power, or the fact I can't run Opus locally. But it doesn't matter - the tool exists in the shape it does, and I have to consume it in the way that it's presented. I hope for a different offering that is more democratic and open, but right now the market hasn't provided that.

It's as if you got access to fiber or broadband and were asked to go back to ISDN/dial up.

nerptastic 2026/04/08 00:25
伙计，我真的以为这是讽刺。令人惊奇的是，您可以在堆栈的所有层获得 10 倍的收益，您必须拥有一个非常小的开发团队或单独工作。

我只是不知道如何在此时导出 10 倍的工作并让同行对其进行正确验证。我可能能够以 10-20 倍的速度生成代码，但在我的特定领域中，存在只有人类才能理解的细微差别。

Man I really thought this was satire. It’s phenomenal that you can gain 10x benefits at all layers of the stack, you must have a very small development team or work alone.

I just don’t see how I could export 10x the work and have it properly validated by peers at this point in time. I may be able to generate code 10-20x faster, but there are nuances that only a human can reason about in my particular sector.

suzzer99 2026/04/08 01:21
拥有25年经验的高级工程师。我希望我花足够的时间实际编码，使我的编码效率提高 10 倍，这对我的工作非常重要。我一天的大部分时间都花在争论需求、照顾初级开发人员、在失控之前扑灭混乱的火灾，并且通常只是试图引导应用程序远离失败。

当我编写代码时，它几乎总是一些新颖的东西，我不知道如何实现，直到我编写了一些代码并查看它们如何组合在一起。如果它是基于现有模式的相当常规的功能，我会将其分配给其他开发人员之一。

Senior engineer with 25 years of experience here. I wish I spent enough time actually coding that 10x-ing my coding productivity would matter much to my job. Most of my day is spent wrangling requirements, looking after junior devs, stamping out confusion brush fires before they get out of control, and generally just trying to steer the app away from a trainwreck down the line.

When I do code, it's almost always something novel that I don't know how I'm going to implement until I code a few pieces and see how they fit together. If it's a fairly routine feature based on an existing pattern, I assign it to one of the other devs.

在 HN 中查看
shuntress 2026/04/08 05:58
这基本上是我在使用代理工具时不断回想起的事情。 这项工作中真正具有挑战性的部分是争论的要求、消除混乱以及避免陷入困境，而我们还无法将这些自动化。一旦您确实知道想要进行的代码更改，尽管以比以前快 10 倍的速度进行更改是非常好的。
This is basically the thing I keep coming back to with the agentic tools. It is the wrangling requirements, stamping out confusion, and steering away from a trainwreck down the line that are the actual challenging parts of the job and we can't automate those yet. Once you do actually know the code change you want to make though it is pretty nice to change it 10x faster than before.
在 HN 中查看
Aurornis 2026/04/08 01:35
> 我只是不知道如何在此时导出 10 倍的工作并让同行对其进行正确验证。

根据我的经验，使用 Claude Code 将输出提高 10 倍的人适合以下两类之一：

1. 他们并没有真正花时间去理解他们提交的代码。他们可能会浏览一下输出，发现它看起来合理并通过测试，但他们不会像结对编程一样花时间来理解代码。只有当它损坏并且法学硕士无法快速修复它时，他们才会深入并完全理解代码。

2. 他们在克劳德·科德面前移动得很慢。我有一些同事需要 2-3 天才能完成一份简单的 PR，因为坦率地说，他们的工作日并没有太多工作。每当他们遇到问题时，他们都会停下来，然后笨手笨脚地呆上几个小时，直到他们可以与票证创建者讨论这个问题。他们会在下午 2 点之前厌倦某项任务，然后把剩下的工作留到明天。他们会得到一个想法，并决定第二天重写 PR，然后继续分心。当他们开始使用 Claude Code 时，LLM 不会遇到同样的问题，所以现在每次他们之前遇到困难或疲倦的地方都会被 LLM 所取代，从而提供某种解决方案。他们的认知负荷减轻了，因此白天不再感到寒冷。他们并没有像他们想象的那样真正成为 10 倍工程师，而只是追赶正常的步伐

> I just don’t see how I could export 10x the work and have it properly validated by peers at this point in time.

In my experience, the people who 10X their output with Claude Code fit one of two categories:

1. They're not really taking the time to understand the code they're submitting. They might do a skim over the output and see that it looks reasonable and passes tests, but they aren't taking time to understand the code as if they were pair programming. Only when it breaks and the LLM can't patch it up quickly do they go in and fully understand the code.

2. They moved very slowly before Claude Code. I've had some coworkers who would take 2-3 days to get a simple PR out because, to be frank, their work days weren't full of a lot of work. Every time they'd run into a question they'd stop and then bumble around for a few hours until they could talk to the ticket creator about it. They'd get tired of working on a task by 2PM and then save the rest of the work for tomorrow. They'd get an idea and decide to rewrite the PR the next day, and on and on with distractions. When they start using Claude Code the LLM doesn't have the same holdups, so now every time where they were getting stuck or tired before is replaced by an LLM powering through to some solution. Their cognitive load is reduced so they're no longer freezing up during the day. They aren't really becoming 10X engineers like they think, but really just catching up to normal pace

在 HN 中查看
rockostrich 2026/04/08 01:53
我不知道我们是否都是 10x'ing，但我们整个组织正在使用类似于 Stripe 的 Minions [1] 的内部框架来发送 PR，并且其中许多 PR 是从 Slack 生成的。我们确实需要在 SDLC 的后半部分做一些工作，以便对这些变化更有信心，但我们仍然可以依靠现有的可观察层来确保事情按预期进行。

另一位评论者提到，Docker、git 等都是大大提高生产力的工具，而编码代理只是实现这一点的另一个工具。我同意，但认为它比所有这些工具的总和更有影响力。

[1] https://stripe.dev/blog/minions-stripes-one-shot-end-to-end-...

I don't know if we're all 10x'ing but our entire org is shipping PRs using an in-house framework akin to Stripe's Minions [1] and many of those PRs are generated from Slack. We definitely have work to do on the latter part of the SDLC to have more confidence in these changes but we can still rely on the existing observability layer to make sure things are working as expected.

Another commenter mentioned that Docker, git, etc. were all tools that greatly enhanced productivity and coding agents are just another tool that does that. I would agree, but argue that it's more impactful than all of those tools combined.

[1] https://stripe.dev/blog/minions-stripes-one-shot-end-to-end-...

hsuduebc2 2026/04/08 06:36
我从你的反对票中看到了一些混乱。肖恩引用了“海豹突击队复制意大利面”的开始

海豹突击队复制粘贴是一种互联网迷因，由一个荒谬的、过分的硬汉威胁组成，发布的内容是为了嘲笑那些在网上表现出某种攻击性、不安全感或自以为是的人，通常是在一场小争吵之后。

这是全文：

>你他妈的刚刚说我什么了，你这个小贱人？我会让你知道，我在海豹突击队中以全班第一的成绩毕业，并且参与了对基地组织的多次秘密突袭，已确认击杀 300 多次。我接受过大猩猩战争训练，是整个美国武装部队中的顶级狙击手。你对我来说什么都不是，只是另一个目标。我会用地球上前所未见的精确度把他妈的消灭掉，记住我他妈的话。你以为你在网上对我说那些狗屎就能逃脱惩罚吗？再想想吧，混蛋。就在我们说话的时候，我正在联系我在美国各地的秘密间谍网络，你的 IP 正在被追踪，所以你最好为风暴做好准备，蛆虫。这场风暴摧毁了你称之为生命的可悲的小东西。你他妈的死定了，孩子。我可以在任何地方，任何时间，我可以用七百多种方式杀死你，而且只是徒手。我不仅接受过广泛的徒手格斗训练，而且我还可以使用美国海军陆战队的全部武器库，我将充分利用它，把你这个可怜的家伙从非洲大陆上抹去，你这个小混蛋。如果你能知道你的小“聪明”评论会给你带来什么邪恶的报应，也许你他妈的就会闭嘴了。但你不能，你没有，现在你正在付出代价，你这个该死的白痴。我会向你发怒，你会被淹没在其中。你他妈死定了，孩子。

I see some confusion from your downvotes. Sean cited the start of "navy seal copypasta"

The Navy Seal copypasta is a internet meme consisting of an absurdly over the top tough guy threat, posted to mock someone who is acting somehow aggressive, insecure, or self important online, usually after a minor argument.

Here it's full text:

>What the fuck did you just fucking say about me, you little bitch? I'll have you know I graduated top of my class in the Navy Seals, and I've been involved in numerous secret raids on Al-Quaeda, and I have over 300 confirmed kills. I am trained in gorilla warfare and I'm the top sniper in the entire US armed forces. You are nothing to me but just another target. I will wipe you the fuck out with precision the likes of which has never been seen before on this Earth, mark my fucking words. You think you can get away with saying that shit to me over the Internet? Think again, fucker. As we speak I am contacting my secret network of spies across the USA and your IP is being traced right now so you better prepare for the storm, maggot. The storm that wipes out the pathetic little thing you call your life. You're fucking dead, kid. I can be anywhere, anytime, and I can kill you in over seven hundred ways, and that's just with my bare hands. Not only am I extensively trained in unarmed combat, but I have access to the entire arsenal of the United States Marine Corps and I will use it to its full extent to wipe your miserable ass off the face of the continent, you little shit. If only you could have known what unholy retribution your little "clever" comment was about to bring down upon you, maybe you would have held your fucking tongue. But you couldn't, you didn't, and now you're paying the price, you goddamn idiot. I will shit fury all over you and you will drown in it. You're fucking dead, kiddo.

Aurornis 2026/04/08 01:22
我经常使用 Claude Code，但我不明白这些“我正在做 10 倍的工作”的评论。

我花了很多时间审查 Claude Code 中的所有代码。即使尽最大努力使用 Opus 4.6，几乎总是有一些东西需要改变，而且通常是巨大的。

我可以看到人们如何思考“哇，这段代码编译并通过了我的测试！发布它！”并开始将信任移交给 Opus，但我已经看到了 6 个月后会发生什么：项目陷入如此复杂的困境和 LLM 意大利面，以至于代码库变得脆弱。每个人都偏离了重组过去混乱的代码，然后与变化中出现的错误作斗争。

我相信最近的一些研究表明法学硕士可以将工作速度提高约 20% (1.2 倍)，因为这与我和其他人在仔细使用时看到的数量级相同。

当有人站出来声称输出增加了 10 倍时，我简直不敢相信他们正在做仔细的工程工作，而不是只是粗略地浏览一下后交付输出。

I use Claude Code a lot, but I don't understand these "I'm doing 10X the work" comments.

I spend a lot of time reviewing any code that comes out of Claude Code. Even using Opus 4.6 with max effort there is almost always something that needs to be changed, often dramatically.

I can see how people go down the path of thinking "Wow, this code compiles and passes my tests! Ship it!" and start handing trust over to Opus, but I've already seen what this turns into 6 months down the road: Projects get mired down in so much complexity and LLM spaghetti that the codebase becomes fragile. Everyone is sidetracked restructuring messy code from the past, then fighting bugs that appear in the change.

I can believe some of the more recent studies showing LLMs can accelerate work by circa 20% (1.2X) because that's on the same order of magnitude that I and others are seeing with careful use.

When someone comes out and claims 10X more output, I simply cannot believe they're doing careful engineering work instead of just shipping the output after a cursory glance.

在 HN 中查看
tracker1 2026/04/08 02:33
我发现这与你花费的规划时间有关...我觉得使用 Claude Code w/ Opus 获得的输出大约是我自己完成的工作的 5 倍...也就是说，我花在规划上的时间可能是直接为自己/自己编码时的 3 倍。 这通常就是区别。

我可以使用代理围绕我正在处理的部分构建大量测试/演示框架，并让代理填写。不过，我仍然花费大量时间来验证测试和正在完成的代码。

我倾向于从代理那里得到的错误与我从远程工作的开发人员/团队那里看到的错误大致相似......您仍然需要验证。 不同之处在于，几天内的转变似乎只需几分钟。您还可以通过简单的审查进行观察...当我看到错误的路径时，我通常可以中止/取消，恢复到上次提交并通过更多计划重试。

I find that it's relative to the amount of planning time you spend... I feel like I've gotten around 5x the output while using Claude Code w/ Opus over what I will get done myself... That said, I'm probably spending about 3x as much time planning as I would when just straight coding for/by myself. And that's generally the difference.

I can use the agent to scaffold a lot of test/demo frameworks around the pieces I'm working on pretty cleanly and have the agent fill in. I still spend a lot of time validating the tests and the code being completed though.

The errors I tend to get from the agent are roughly similar to what I might see from a developer/team that works remotely... you still need to verify. The difference is the turn around seems to be minutes over days. You're also able to observe over simply review... When I see a bad path, I can usually abort/cancel, revert back to the last commit and try again with more planning.

在 HN 中查看
Pay08 2026/04/08 05:53
这就是为什么我根本不接受人工智能来直接编写代码的部分原因。如果我要回顾其中的任何内容（我会的），那么我还不如自己写。它更容易、更快，尽管它也更容易成为盲点的受害者。
That's part of why I don't get AI for directly writing code at all. If I am going to be reviewing anything that comes out of it (and I will) then I might as well just write it myself. It's easier and faster, although it does also make it easier to fall victim to blind spots.
在 HN 中查看
eijkene 2026/04/08 07:24
我认为有一部分工程师从来都不是那么优秀（我们不知道有多少人），他们从法学硕士中受益最多。

我们还应该记住，高质量的开发人员一直严重短缺。所以我对我们所看到的并不感到惊讶。

但精英开发人员的生产力提高了 10 倍的观点绝对是无稽之谈。在大多数情况下，法学硕士都会阻碍专家的发展。

I think there’s a subset of engineers who were never all that good (we have no idea how many there are) who benefit most from llm’s.

We should also keep in mind there’s always been an insane shortage of high quality devs. So I’m not surprised with what we seeing.

But this notion that an elite dev is seeing 10x productivity gain is absolute nonsense. LLM’s hold experts back in most contexts.

xantronix 2026/04/08 01:01
介意我将来用它作为复制意大利面吗？ 这会检查人们在 LinkedIn 和其他地方提出的每一个观点。

但认真地说，编写代码，甚至坐下来正确构建事物，对我来说从来都不是瓶颈。 要么是人为的截止日期阻止我编写适当的单元测试，要么是我的团队中的人员要求进行代码审查，而他们甚至不像我每天使用相同的代码库。 我经常声明并坚持这样的说法：我以自己理解的速度发展，我认为这是一种很好的美德，我认为这种美德将经受住时间的考验，并带来最好的组织成果。 问题只是找到重视这种方法的合适地方。

编辑上下文：我的团队是一个需要几个开发人员的运维团队；我被选去实施一些内部工具。 我最初开发的最后期限与我的绩效评估直接相关。 我的老板担任经理才近两年。 他担任开发人员的时间还不到一年。 他本人从未加入过开发团队。 这个人不会休息，并且抓住每一个机会进行微观管理。 他对自己的工作很偏执，认为他将很快被我们（更便宜的）欧盟同行取代。 他的管理风格和口头警告反映了这一点；他经常使用不必要的指责性言论，将这些不安全感投射到他人身上。 我并不是团队中唯一与他有过此类互动的开发人员。 我有与他对话的屏幕截图，我认为有必要将其呈现给治疗师。 这种程度的时间压力在我20年的职业生涯中是前所未有的。 是的，这是一个功能失调的环境。

Mind if I use this as a copypasta for the future? This checks off every point people bring on LinkedIn and elsewhere.

In all seriousness though, writing code, or even sitting down and properly architecting things, have never been bottlenecks for me. It has either been artificial deadlines preventing me from writing proper unit tests, or the requirement for code review from people on my team who don't even work on the same codebase as I do on a daily basis. I have often stated and stand by the assertion that I develop at the speed of my own understanding, and I think that is a good virtue to carry forth that I think will stand the test of time and bring about the best organisational outcomes. It's just a matter of finding the right place that values this approach.

Edit for context: My team is an ops team that needed a couple developers; I was picked to implement some internal tooling. The deadlines I was given for the initial development are tied directly to my performance evaluation. My boss has only ever been a manager for almost two years. He has only ever had development headcount for less than a year. He has never been on a development team himself. The man does not take breaks and micromanages at every opportunity he gets. He is paranoid for his job, thinking he is going to be imminently replaced by our (cheaper) EU counterparts. His management style and verbal admonitions reflect this; he frequently projects these insecurities onto others, using unnecessarily accusatory speech. I am not the only developer on my team who has had such interactions with him. I have screenshots of conversations with him that I felt necessary to present to a therapist. This degree of time pressure is entirely unprecedented in my 20 year career. Yes, this is a dysfunctional environment.

dwaltrip 2026/04/08 01:04
深入了解您的流程会很酷。您应该记录一些会话:)

我大多相信你。我已经看到了你所说的内容的暗示。

但很多时候我觉得自己走在正确的轨道上，但实际上我只是在旋转，而人工智能也很乐意与之配合。

或者我对某件事太过深入，陷入了循环，脱离了代码的现实和具体问题。

如果我注意到这一点并且不太累，我可以把它卷回来并重新磨碎东西。退后一步，确保我们走在合理的道路上。

但我意识到有时要尽早抓住这个循环是非常困难的。至少对我来说。

我还用它做了一些非常棒的事情，如果没有人工智能，这些事情要么永远不会发生，要么需要更长的时间——在很多情况下很容易达到 5 倍到 10 倍。这一切都非常令人着迷。

有很多东西需要学习。我正在形成这样的想法：发展良好的“人工智能纪律”非常重要。

附注有时我也会有一种奇怪的“人工智能疲惫”的感觉。发送另一个提示的想法感觉非常痛苦。上周我有很多这样的感受。

附注当然，这甚至不涉及随着时间的推移保持代码质量。当法学硕士实施某些事情时的“之后”部分。有很多好的模式和方法可以处理这个问题，但这是该过程的一个独特阶段，有很多复杂性和细微差别。而且很容易跳过或推迟。如果人工智能输出更大——就在你最需要的时候，情况更是如此。

It’d be cool to see your process in depth. You should record some of your sessions :)

I mostly believe you. I have seen hints of what you are talking about.

But often times I feel like I’m on the right track but I’m actually just spinning when wheels and the AI is just happily going along with it.

Or I’m getting too deep on something and I’m caught up in the loop, becoming ungrounded from the reality of the code and the specific problem.

If I notice that and am not too tired, I can reel it back in and re-ground things. Take a step back and make sure we are on reasonable path.

But I’m realizing it can be surprisingly difficult to catch that loop early sometimes. At least for me.

I’ve also done some pretty awesome shit with it that either would have never happened or taken far longer without AI — easily 5x-10x in many cases. It’s all quite fascinating.

Much to learn. This idea is forming for me that developing good “AI discipline” is incredibly important.

P.s. sometimes I also get this weird feeling of “AI exhaustion”. Where the thought of sending another prompt feels quite painful. The last week I’ve felt that a lot.

P.p.s. And then of course this doesn’t even touch on maintaining code quality over time. The “after” part when the LLM implements something. There are lots of good patterns and approaches for handling this, but it’s a distinct phase of the process with lots of complexities and nuances. And it’s oh-so-temping to skip or postpone. More so if the AI output is larger — exactly when you need it most.

在 HN 中查看
ericmcer 2026/04/08 00:22
我的意思是，在这一点上，我们是否可以得出这样的结论：有一群工程师声称使用它取得了令人难以置信的成功，而另一群则声称它不可靠并且不能被信任来完成复杂的任务。

我很难相信大量看似聪明的软件工程师太愚蠢了，无法弄清楚如何使用 Claude 代码来获得可靠的结果，在我看来，它更有可能在孤立的任务或新项目中表现出色，但在大型复杂的代码库中却失败了，因为它只是......只是一个令牌预测器，哈哈。

但是，是的，在广泛解决的领域（分类账）中启动一个绿色领域项目将是人工智能的亮点。

我们也不是不使用这些东西，我要求 Cursor 每天做 20 次事情，而它有 50% 的时间做我不喜欢的事情。甚至像粘贴错误消息这样的事情也很难解决。如何协调我的实际日常体验和我在网上看到的炒作信息？

I mean at this point can we just conclude that there are a group of engineers who claim to have incredible success with it and a group that claim it is unreliable and cannot be trusted to do complex tasks.

I struggle to believe that a ton of seemingly intelligent software engineers are too dumb to figure out how to use Claude code to get reliable results, it seems much more likely to me that it can do well at isolated tasks or new projects but fails when pointed at large complex code bases because it just... is a token predictor lol.

But yeah spinning up a green fields project in an extensively solved area (ledgers) is going to be something an AI shines at.

It isn't like we don't use this stuff also, I ask Cursor to do things 20x a day and it does something I don't like 50% of the time. Even things like pasting an error message it struggles with. How do I reconcile my actual daily experience with hype messages I see online?

在 HN 中查看
rurp 2026/04/08 00:50
是的，我经常看到人们以同样的方式互相交谈。当人们说他们使用 Claude 将一些新项目的编码速度提高了 10 倍时，我并不怀疑，它显然在许多任务上都表现出色！但他们中的许多人声称他们的经验应该转化为每个场景中的每个开发人员，甚至说如果他们没有相同的经验，他们一定是错误地使用它。

许多软件开发人员在大型项目中以团队形式工作，其中法学硕士具有更细微的价值。我本人主要在大型组织内的大型项目上工作。对我来说，吐出几行代码实际上从来都不是瓶颈。运行一套代理来生成大量代码供我的同事审查并不能真正解决我遇到的问题。我仍然以其他方式使用 Claude，并发现它很有用，但我的工作效率肯定没有提高 10 倍。

Right, I keep seeing people talking past each other in this same way. I don't doubt folks when they say they coded up some greenfield project 10x faster with Claude, it's clearly great at many of those tasks! But then so many of them claim that their experience should translate to every developer in every scenario, to the point of saying they must be using it wrong if they aren't having the same experience.

Many software devs work in teams on large projects where LLMs have a more nuanced value. I myself mostly work on a large project inside a large organization. Spitting out lines of code is practically never a bottleneck for me. Running a suite of agents to generate out a ton of code for my coworkers to review doesn't really solve a problem that I have. I still use Claude in other ways and find it useful, but I'm certainly not 10x more productive with it.

在 HN 中查看
dns_snek 2026/04/08 01:26
> 但是，是的，在广泛解决的领域（分类账）中启动一个绿色领域项目将是人工智能的亮点。

我非常不同意这一点。它在构建演示方面令人印象深刻，但根据我的经验，要求它为长期项目奠定基础是灾难性的。

当你有一个已建立的项目并且你要求它在线条之间着色时，它可以做得很好（大多数时候），但是当你给它一个空白画布和大量的自主权时，它可能最终会以惊人的速度生成垃圾代码。这变成了一场与熵的持续斗争，你没有立即清理的每一个混乱都会被视为下一次“应该做的事情”。

在有人问之前，这是我对 Claude Code (Sonnet/Opus 4.6) 和 Codex (GPT 5.4) 的体验。

> But yeah spinning up a green fields project in an extensively solved area (ledgers) is going to be something an AI shines at.

I couldn't disagree with this more. It's impressive at building demos, but asking it to build the foundation for a long-term project has been disastrous in my experience.

When you have an established project and you're asking it to color between the lines it can do that well (most of the time), but when you give it a blank canvas and a lot of autonomy it will likely end up generating crap code at a staggering pace. It becomes a constant fight against entropy where every mess you don't clean up immediately gets picked up as "the way things should be done" the next time.

Before someone asks, this is my experience with both Claude Code (Sonnet/Opus 4.6) and Codex (GPT 5.4).

在 HN 中查看
hombre_fatal 2026/04/08 00:26
我怀疑这里的很多人都尝试过它，但他们希望它能够一次性发出任何提示，而当它没有出现时，它证实了他们想要的真实情况，他们的反应是“哈，看到了吗？”然后洗手。

所以并不是他们太傻。这样做的动机有多种：坚持熟悉、抵制感觉像是另一种工具、反人工智能 koolaid、真心不知所措但不明白它还能好多少、对他们认为是不断拉拉队的东西做出反应，等等。

这有点像 10 多年前 HN 上的反 JavaScript 帖子。这些人并没有愚蠢到无法理解如何使用 Steelman Node.js，他们只是没有足够的好奇心来询问，也许结果证明他们甚至没有使用过 Javascript，因为“DHTML”是一个除了执行 $(“.box”).toggle() 之外的术语。

我希望人们对 HN 有更多好奇。

I suspect many people here have tried it, but they expected it to one-shot any prompt, and when it didn't, it confirmed what they wanted to be true and they responded with "hah, see?" and then washed their hands of it.

So it's not that they're too stupid. There are various motivations for this: clinging on to familiarity, resistance to what feels like yet another tool, anti-AI koolaid, earnestly underwhelmed but don't understand how much better it can be, reacting to what they perceive to be incessant cheerleading, etc.

It's kind of like anti-Javascript posts on HN 10+ years ago. These people weren't too stupid to understand how you could steelman Node.js, they just weren't curious enough to ask, and maybe it turned out they hadn't even used Javascript since "DHTML" was a term except to do $(".box").toggle().

I wish there were more curiosity on HN.

在 HN 中查看
ericmcer 2026/04/08 00:44
那么我该怎么做呢？

假设，您会遇到一个简单的切片越界错误，因为函数正在获取空字符串，因此它会执行类似以下操作：`""[5]`。

Opus 将添加一堆长度和 nil 检查来“修复”这个问题，但实际问题是字符串永远不应该为空。 nil 检查只是掩盖了更深层次的问题，就像您可能需要对最小字符串长度进行架构级别检查一样。

到那时，你是否只是告诉它“不要删除所有这些，字符串永远不应该为空”并让它弄清楚，或者我基本上需要伪代码“在第145行向此文件添加空字符串检查”，或者我只是YOLO并知道问题现在已经消失，所以它不再是我的问题？

我更重要的观点是法学硕士如何知道这个看似很小的问题预示着一些更大的失败，比如这个字符串是 `user.username` 这意味着用户可以将他们的名字设置为空，这意味着可能需要整个迁移。人工智能要做的就是消除错误消息并解决问题。

So what do I do differently then?

Hypothetically, you have a simple slice out of bounds error because a function is getting an empty string so it does something like: `""[5]`.

Opus will add a bunch of length & nil checks to "fix" this, but the actual issue is the string should never be empty. The nil checks are just papering over a deeper issue, like you probably need a schema level check for minimum string length.

At that point do you just tell it like "no delete all that, the string should never be empty" and let it figure that out, or do I basically need to pseudo code "add a check for empty strings to this file on line 145", or do I just YOLO and know the issue is gone now so it is no longer my problem?

My bigger point is how does an LLM know that this seemingly small problem is indicative of some larger failure, like lets say this string is a `user.username` which means users can set their name to empty which means an entire migration is probably necessary. All the AI is going to do is smoosh the error messages and kick the can.

在 HN 中查看
UI_at_80x24 2026/04/08 01:10
我也遇到过完全相同的挫败感，而且没有任何提示似乎可以阻止它“随机”发生。

`the error is on line #145 fix it with XYZ and add a check that no string should ever be blank`

令人沮丧的是随机性，而且手动输入修复会更快，这让我发疯。 我担心我添加到 claude.md 的所有“规则”都会浪费我的可用令牌，它没有足够的空间来处理我的请求。

I have encountered the exact same kind of frustration, and no amount of prompting seems to prevent it from "randomly" happening.

`the error is on line #145 fix it with XYZ and add a check that no string should ever be blank`

It's the randomness that is frustrating, and that the fix would be quicker to manually input that drives me crazy. I fear that all the "rules" I add to claude.md is wasting my available tokens it won't have enough room to process my request.

在 HN 中查看
unshavedyak 2026/04/08 01:21
是的，这就是为什么我坚信真正的生产力，因为它可以帮助你提高速度，但受到审阅速度的限制。

我认为 Claude 让我更快，但斗争始终集中在保留自己的上下文和全面审查代码上。全面审查代码以确保其正确且符合我想要的方式，保留我自己的上下文以加快审查速度并且不会迷失方向。

我坚信那些看到巨大收益的人只是忽略了 x% 行代码。有一个论点认为这是可以接受的，但目前这是一个风险分析问题。没有一本是我订阅的。

Yup, this is why i firmly believe true productivity, as in, it aiding you to make you faster, is limited by the speed of review.

I think Claude makes me faster, but the struggle is always centered around retaining own context and reviewing code fully. Reviewing code fully to make sure it’s correct and the way I want it, retaining my own context to speed up reviews and not get lost.

I firmly believe people who are seeing massive gains are simply ignoring x% lines of code. There’s an argument to be made for that being acceptable, but it’s a risk analysis problem currently. Not one I subscribe to.

在 HN 中查看
julian37 2026/04/08 01:21
使用计划+执行而不是一次性，它会让你推迟这样的事情。我建议集思广益 https://github.com/obra/superpowers，至少一开始是这样。

然后努力确保法学硕士拥有所需的所有信息。在此示例中，听起来您的假设数据模型可能需要更好地键入和/或记录。

但是，是的，从今天开始，它不会像你一样识别气味，至少在没有额外技能/提示的情况下。你会发现这令人安慰或烦人，这取决于你所处的位置......

Use planning+execution rather than one-shotting, it'll let you push back on stuff like this. I recommend brainstorming everything with https://github.com/obra/superpowers, at least to start with.

Then work on making sure the LLM has all the info it needs. In this example it sounds like perhaps your hypothetical data model would need to be better typed and/or documented.

But yeah as of today it won't pick up on smells as you do, at least not without extra skills/prompting. You'll find that comforting or annoying depending on where you stand...

在 HN 中查看
hombre_fatal 2026/04/08 02:20
始终以 Claude Code 计划模式开始实施。它比直接实现要全面得多。我以前从未读过他们对计划模式的提示，但它深入探讨了代码、外围文件、调用站点、文档、现有测试等。

您不仅可以获得更好的解决方案，还可以获得可以查看的计划文件。而且，同样重要的是，请另一位代理人进行审查。我发现 Codex 非常擅长审查计划。

我有一个 AGENTS.md 提示，它解释了计划文件审查涉及按严重性对最重要的发现进行排名、解释影响以及对每个发现提出修复建议。最后推荐一种更简单的方向枢轴（如果该计划存在）。

因此，在 Claude Code 中启动计划，在 Codex（或另一个 Claude Code 代理）中输入“查看此计划：<路径>”，然后将结果循环回 Claude Code 中以完善计划。更新计划后，将“计划已更新”写给审阅者代理。

通过这种能力更好的架构级别更改，而不是狭隘的主题解决方案，您应该获得更好的结果。

如果这对您来说仍然不够有效，也许您可​​以使用更多支持，例如 AGENTS.md 中的类型系统和更多目标？

Always start an implementation in Claude Code plan mode. It's much more comprehensive than going straight to impl. I never read their prompt for plan mode before, but it deep-dives the code, peripheral files, callsites, documentation, existing tests, etc.

You get a better solution but also a plan file that you can review. And, also important, have another agent review. I've found that Codex is really good at reviewing plans.

I have an AGENTS.md prompt that explains that plan file review involves ranking the top findings by severity, explaining the impact, and recommending a fix to each one. And finally recommend a simpler directional pivot if one exists for the plan.

So, start the plan in Claude Code, type "Review this plan: <path>" in Codex (or another Claude Code agent), and cycle the findings back into Claude Code to refine the plan. When the plan is updated, write "Plan updated" to the reviewer agent.

You should get much better results with this capable of much better arch-level changes rather than narrow topical solutions.

If that's still not working sufficiently for you, maybe you could use more support, like a type-system and more goals in AGENTS.md?

在 HN 中查看
skydhash 2026/04/08 03:08
IMO，计划模式毫无用处。对于错误修复和小的改进，我已经知道在哪里进行编辑（并且可以使用 vim-fu 快速完成）。

对于新功能，我会花一些时间思考，并且通常可以将其分解为易于编码和验证的较小任务。无需纠结计划模式和大的降价文件。

如果我麻烦经纪人的话，到那时我通常可以一次性解决问题。

IMO, plan mode is pretty useless. For bug fixes and small improvements, I already know where to edit (and can do it quickly with vim-fu).

For new features, I spend a bit of time thinking, and I can usually break it down in smaller tasks that are easy to code and verify. No need to wrangle with Plan mode and a big markdown file.

I can usually get things one-shotted by that point if I bother with the agent.

在 HN 中查看
dpkirchner 2026/04/08 01:05
不是你要回复的人，但是的，有时我确实会告诉代理人删除这些残渣。然后我在上下文中备份了一些消息并重新表述了我的请求。我不只是说“修复这个崩溃”，或者其他什么，我说“这会崩溃，因为字符串是空的，但它不应该是空的，弄清楚为什么它是空的”。我可能会添加一些测试，以确保任何代码都不会返回/传递空字符串。
Not the person you're replying to but yes, sometimes I do tell the agent to remove the cruft. Then I back up a few messages in the context and reword my request. Instead of just saying "fix this crash", or whatever, I say "this is crashing because the string is empty, however it shouldn't be empty, figure out why it's empty". And I might have it add some tests to ensure that whatever code is not returning/passing along empty strings.
在 HN 中查看
echelon 2026/04/08 01:53
1. 我使用 Rust 工作，所以它是一种非常安全且低缺陷的语言。我怀疑这与我的成功有很大关系。 “空值”(Option<T>) 和“错误”(Result<T,E>) 必须 AST 编码了大量关于状态、流程以及如何处理事物的信息。我对 Claude Code 的 TypeScript 和 React 输出感觉不太舒服 - 它们确实有效，但可能更加不精确。而且我只信任新手 Python，编辑现有的 Python 代码一直很草率。 Rust 的体验非常神奇。

2. 我从架构上描述了我想要做的每一个改变。我不会让法学硕士来猜测。我的提示可能有点过头了，但一次性就可以达到 70-80% 的正确率。 （我还没有测量过这一点，我实际上很好奇。）我将粘贴文件路径、方法名称、结构定义，并询问 Claude 进行具体更改。我将根据需要将“通过查询和 API 层检测 foo 字段”扩展为尽可能详细的内容。我的提示可能有几个段落。

3. 我不会通过单个提示尝试整个变更集或 PR。我像自然地工作一样迭代地工作，只是在更高的层次上、更大更广的范围内。一段时间后，你就会了解克劳德可以在什么粒度和范围上发挥作用。

你不能一枪毙命。你必须迭代地工作。单个 PR 可能是增量更改的多次往返。这就像成为一名“电影导演”或“结对程序员”编写代码。我有严格的规格和指示。

其力量在于这些改变的速度有多快以及它们与您的期望的吻合程度。还有它消耗你的精力和注意力的程度。

这也让我有机会在每次更改时进行代码审查，这意味着当我审查最终 PR 时，我已经多次阅读了更改集。

1. I'm working in Rust, so it's a very safe and low-defect language. I suspect that has a tremendous amount to do with my successes. "nulls" (Option<T>) and "errors" (Result<T,E>) must be handled, and the AST encodes a tremendous amount about the state, flow, and how to deal with things. I do not feel as comfortable with Claude Code's TypeScript and React outputs - they do work, but it can be much more imprecise. And I only trust it with greenfield Python, editing existing Python code has been sloppy. The Rust experience is downright magical.

2. I architecturally describe every change I want made. I don't leave it up to the LLM to guess. My prompts might be overkill, but they result in 70-80ish% correctness in one shot. (I haven't measured this, and I'm actually curious.) I'll paste in file paths, method names, struct definitions and ask Claude for concrete changes. I'll expand "plumb foo field through the query and API layers" into as much detail as necessary. My prompts can be several paragraphs in length.

3. I don't attempt an entire change set or PR with a single prompt. I work iteratively as I would naturally work, just at a higher level and with greater and broader scope. You get a sense of what granularity and scope Claude can be effective at after a while.

You can't one shot stuff. You have to work iteratively. A single PR might be multiple round trips of incremental change. It's like being a "film director" or "pair programmer" writing code. I have exacting specifications and directions.

The power is in how fast these changes can be made and how closely they map to your expectations. And also in how little it drains your energy and focus.

This also gives me a chance to code review at every change, which means by the time I review the final PR, I've read the change set multiple times.

在 HN 中查看
alwillis 2026/04/08 01:03
> 我很难相信大量看似聪明的软件工程师太笨了，无法弄清楚如何使用 Claude 代码来获得可靠的结果。

他们并不愚蠢，但他们的挣扎我并不感到惊讶。

在将人工智能添加到组合中时，开发人员的思维方式必须改变，而许多开发人员要么不能，要么不会这样做。提交看起来像“修复了一些错误”的开发人员可能也不会花时间写一个像样的提示。

每当技术发生转变时，总会有人无法或不愿适应。老实说，有些人的议程（有意或无意）是维持现状并“证明”人工智能是一件坏事。

难怪我们会看到关于编码代理有效性的截然不同的故事。

> I struggle to believe that a ton of seemingly intelligent software engineers are too dumb to figure out how to use Claude code to get reliable results.

They're not dumb, but I'm not surprised they're struggling.

A developer's mindset has to change when adding AI into the mix, and many developers either can’t or won’t do that. Developers whose commits that look something like "Fixed some bugs" probably aren’t going to take the time to write a decent prompt either.

Whenever there's a technology shift, there are always people who can't or won't adapt. And let's be honest, there are folks whose agenda (consciously or not) is to keep the status quo and "prove" that AI is a bad thing.

No wonder we're seeing wildly different stories about the effectiveness of coding agents.

在 HN 中查看
jerf 2026/04/08 01:50
对于那些声称它在这一点上毫无用处的人来说，我看到了两个基本案例。

一个是他们在一两年前尝试过基于人工智能的编码，当时得出的结论是完全正确的：恕我直言，它几乎毫无用处，从那以后就没有尝试过，看看情况是否有所改变。解决办法是什么，再试一次。它改变了很多。

另一种是那些已经将讨厌人工智能并且永远不会使用它的人融入到他们的个人身份中。我见过人们在一项他们有充分理由相信人工智能会失败的任务上做一些事情，当它失败时，将其投射到所有任务上，而不让自己有意识地意识到故意选择一个糟糕的任务会扭曲整个计划。

对于这些人，我的解决方案是鼓励他们坚持怀疑态度。尽管有令人难以置信的认知诱惑，我也试图坚持下去。这非常有用。但与此同时……是的，在过去一年左右的时间里发生了一步变化。它变得更加有用......

...但很多实用性并不会排除熟练的高级编码技能。它喜欢编写没有强类型的脚本代码。自从我上次写这篇文章以来，我实际上已经在这样的情况下使用了它：有足够多的强类型，它自发地产生了一些，但无论它使用什么语言，它仍然倾向于在该上下文之外编写脚本代码。它擅长非常直接的代码解决方案，但我很少看到它建议使用数据库，或事件源，或消息总线，或任何其他东西......它有很多“这里没有发明综合症”，它反而会提出一些通过单元测试的最小解决方案表现出色，但无法大规模部署。无论项目有多少文档，它通常都会重复代码，因为上下文窗口太大，并且它不一定知道重复代码可能在哪里。它仍然需要各种方式的帮助才能产生良好的输出。

我还想知道有多少人未能充分提示这一点。我的一些提示基本上是“采取这个、执行那个并编写一个函数来记录错误”，但是我的很多提示都是项目的相关上下文的一个屏幕或两个屏幕，我们正在尝试做什么，为什么明显的解决方案不起作用，这里有一些其他代码需要查看，这里有相关的错误和一些关于项目规划的 Wiki 文档，我们应该使用{事件源/不可变树/存储过程/任何}，在开始之前与我互动以解答问题任何东西。这并不是对他们正在做的事情的完整解释，但仍然有很多方法可以让法学硕士真正可以做的是 风格转移...它只是“采取这个并执行那个并编写一个函数来记录错误”并将其样式转换为源代码。如果你想让它做一些有趣的事情，那么首先给它足够的信息，让“风格转移”掌握并做一些事情，确实很有帮助。不要觉得“向计算机解释它”很愚蠢，您正在为函数提供足够的数据来进行操作。

I see two basic cases for the people who are claiming it is useless at this point.

One is that they tried AI-based coding a year or two ago, came to the IMHO completely correct at that time conclusion that it was nearly useless, and have not tried it since then to see that the situation has changed. To which the solution is, try it again. It changed a lot.

The other are those who have incorporated into their personal identity that they hate AI and will never use it. I have seen people do things like fire AI at a task they have good reasons to believe it will fail at, and when it does, project that out to all tasks without letting themselves consciously realize that picking a bad task on purpose skews the deck.

To those people my solution is to encourage them to hold on to their skepticism. I try to hold on to it as well despite the incredible cognitive temptation not to. It is very useful. But at the same time... yeah, there was a step change in the past year or so. It has gotten a lot more useful...

... but a lot of that utility is in ways that don't obviate skilled senior coding skills. It likes to write scripting code without strong types. Since the last time I wrote that, I have in fact used it in a situation where there were enough strong types that it spontaneously originated some, but it still tends to write scripting code out of that context no matter what language it is working in. It is good at very straight-line solutions to code but I rarely see it suggest using databases, or event sourcing, or a message bus, or any of a lot of other things... it has a lot of Not Invented Here syndrome where it instead bashes out some minimal solution that passes the unit tests with flying colors but can't be deployed at scale. No matter how much documentation a project has it often ends up duplicating code just because the context window is only so large and it doesn't necessarily know where the duplicated code might be. There's all sorts of ways it still needs help to produce good output.

I also wonder how many people are failing to prompt it enough. Some of my prompts are basically "take this and do that and write a function to log the error", but a lot of my prompts are a screen or two of relevant context of the project, what it is we are trying to do, why the obvious solution doesn't work, here's some other code to look at, here's the relevant bugs and some Wiki documentation on the planning of the project, we should use {event sourcing/immutable trees/stored procedures/whatever}, interact with me for questions before starting anything. This is not a complete explanation of what they are doing anymore, but there's still a lot of ways in which what an LLM can really do is style transfer... it is just taking "take this and do that and write a function to log the error" and style-transforming that into source code. If you want it to do something interesting it really helps to give it enough information in the first place for the "style transfer" to get a hold of and do something with. Don't feel silly "explaining it to a computer", you're giving the function enough data to operate on.

在 HN 中查看
sutib 2026/04/08 04:43
我可以看到人工智能作为指导和帮助者的巨大效用。

但我自己不参与代码并不是一件让我感到舒服的事情。它开始感觉像是管理而不是开发。我真的对这种放弃感到非常强烈，这让我无法也不愿意在质量上留下深刻的印记。我看到了太多的幻觉或一半错过了对人工智能的信任。

硬票的代码审查也是如此。您可以滚动浏览并批准，但您真的了解您的同事构建的内容吗？你是 真的 在驾驶座上？在我看来，这就像 YOLOing 带来的重大后果。

但我不知道，那些进行 20 倍输出的人根本不知道他们在编码什么。他们只是按下 yolo 按钮，没有人（工程师、人工智能和管理层）坐在驾驶座上。这是一个非常可怕的时刻。

I can see huge utility with AI as a guide and helper.

But not being one leg in the code myself is not something I am comfortable with. It starts feeling like management and not development. I really feel the abdication very strongly and it makes me unable and unwilling to put a hard stamp on quality. I have seen too much hallucination or half missed requirements to put that much trust in AI.

It's the same with code reviews of hard tickets. You can scroll past and just approve, but do you really understand what your colleague has built? Are you really in the driver's seat? It feels to me like YOLOing with major consequences.

I dont but, at all that people doing 20x output have any idea what they are coding. They are just pressing the yolo button and no one, not the engineer, not the AI and not management is in the driver's seat. it is a very scary time.

我不想听起来像是我在试图与你一较高下，但我基本上已经对整个互联网进行了振动编码。

我很惊讶在我之前没有人想到这一点，但基本上法学硕士都是在互联网上接受培训的，而我只是让它吐出所有内容。

它是并行运行的，所以我可以验证它，当然我正在使用法学硕士来做到这一点。

一旦准备好，我会将其投放市场，但是得到这个，我的互联网将比当前的互联网便宜。 我可能会便宜一点，比如如果当前的互联网费用是 7，我会让我的互联网费用为 6。

I don't want to sound like I'm trying to one-up you, but I've basically vibe coded the entire internet.

Once it's ready I will put it on the market, but get this, my internet will be cheaper than the current internet. I'll probably just make it one cheaper, like if the current internet costs, for example, 7, I'll make my internet cost 6.

epistasis 2026/04/08 00:17
我仍在审查创建的所有代码，并要求修改，并且基本上使用法学硕士作为 2000 WPM 的打字员，并看到类似的生产力提升。尤其是在新框架中！一切都是测试驱动开发，超级干净且超级快速。

现在的挑战是如何规划架构和代码库，使其变得真正庞大和规模化，而不会让人工智能造成隐藏的技术债务。

代码的基础必须非常坚实，并且从一开始的架构就必须是正确的。但即使重做架构现在也变得更快了......

I'm still reviewing all the code that's created, and asking for modifications, and basically using LLMs as a 2000 wpm typist, and seeing similar productivity gains. Especially in new frameworks! Everything is test driven development, super clean and super fast.

The challenge now is how to plan architectures and codebases to get really big and really scale, without AI slop creating hidden tech debt.

Foundations of the code must be very solid, and the architecture from the start has to be right. But even redoing the architecture becomes so much faster now...

wongarsu 2026/04/08 00:30
除了 Claude Code 订阅（或 Codex 或 Copilot 的同等订阅）之外，还有哪些合理的选择？

我可以为相同的型号支付 API 价格，但除了为相同的结果支付更多费用之外，这似乎没有帮助

我可以支付 4-5 位数的硬件费用来运行远不如开放模型

我可以支付六位数的硬件费用来运行一个功能仅落后几个月的开放模型（或者支付 4-5 位数的费用以蜗牛般的速度运行同一模型）

我可以向半可信推理提供商支付 API 成本来运行这些开放模型之一

这些似乎都不是很好的选择。如果我想要尖端的编码性能，那么订阅是最合理的选择

请注意，这主要适用于编码。对于许多其他任务，本地模型或开放模型的付费推理是非常合理的。但对于编码而言，最后一点性能很重要

What are the reasonable options besides a Claude Code subscription (or an equivalent from Codex or Copilot)?

I could pay API prices for the same models, but aside from paying much more for the same result that doesn't seem helpful

I could pay a 4-5 figure sum for hardware to run a far inferior open model

I could pay a six figure sum for hardware to run an open model that's only a couple months behind in capability (or a 4-5 figure sum to run the same model at a snail's pace)

I could pay API costs to semi-trustworthy inference provider to run one of those open models

None of those seem like great alternatives. If I want cutting-edge coding performance then a subscription is the most reasonable option

Note that this applies mostly to coding. For many other tasks local models or paid inference on open models is very reasonable. But for coding that last bit of performance matters

embedding-shape 2026/04/08 00:24
作为专业人士，您可以选择如何生产您生产的产品。当然，您可以选择最简单、最昂贵和“最容易”的做事方式，也可以做其他事情，具体取决于您的观点和要求。这一切都不是一成不变的，有些人根据个人喜好做出选择，这对他们来说和你的选择对你一样重要。
As a professional you have a choice in how you produce whatever it is you produce. Sure, you can go for the simplest, most expensive and "easiest" way of doing things, or you can do other things, depending on your perspective and requirements. None of this is set in stone, some people make choices based on personal preferences, and that matters as much to them as your choices matter to you.

britzkopf 2026/04/08 00:28

在 HN 中查看
dmoy 2026/04/08 00:48
我的意思是，对于很多问题空间来说，五个 9 确实很难实现。

这也就像......很难诚实和准确地测量。 并根据您的底层依赖项（服务器等）是否像广告中那样崩溃，或者是否实际上是五个九来考虑您是否幸运。 或者，无论您运行了一个月并获得了 <30 秒的停机时间并宣告胜利，还是运行了三年并进行了大量的软件更新。

我总是假设大多数声称五个九的人只是没有正确测量它，或者没有执行在足够长的时间内会出现问题的全套事情（直流故障、网络分区、配置错误、仅在某些端口上丢弃 UDP 流量的不良网络交换机、错误的 ACL 更改、不良的软件更新等）

也许他们做的都是正确的，在这种情况下，是的，对我来说似乎很难。

I mean five nines is legitimately difficult to accomplish for a lot of problem spaces.

It's also like.... difficult to honestly and accurately measure. And account for whether or not you're getting lucky based on your underlying dependencies (servers, etc) not crashing as much as advertised, or if it's actually five nines. Or whether you've run it for a month and gotten <30s of measure downtime and declared victory, vs run it for three years with copious software updates.

I always assume most people claiming five nines are just not measuring it correctly, or have not exercised the full set of things that will go wrong over a long enough period of time (dc failures, network partitions, config errors, bad network switches that drop only UDP traffic on certain ports, erroneous ACL changes, bad software updates, etc etc)

Maybe they did it all correct though, in which case, yea, seems hard hitting to me.

5 nines is at best a temporary achievement, given enough time.

在 HN 中查看
jlokier 2026/04/08 02:39
> 张开双臂拥抱订阅开发工具（实际上是服务），这些工具试图减轻行为本身的负担，这让我想知道如何以及为什么这么多人渴望直接投入其中

这是一个不在您的列表中的原因。

简短版本：一种同侪压力，但是来自上面。 在某些圈子里，我被告知开发人员现在必须在简历中包含人工智能技能，而这些技能可能需要使用众所周知的订阅服务，否则会大大降低他们的就业前景。

我认识的许多雇主最近在没有提示的情况下告诉我，他们不再雇用在工作流程中不使用人工智能的开发人员。

其中一位告诉我，他们认识的所有雇主都认为“资深人士”分为两个阵营，一类是拥抱人工智能，因此灵活且适应性强的人，另一类是回避人工智能，因此过于落后、固守自己的方式，无法成为未来的好员工。 因此，如果他们现在没有在高级开发人员的简历上看到人工智能使用的迹象，那就会自动放弃。 对于我所知道的从研发公司解雇的开发人员来说，人工智能不允许进行开发（出于知识产权/保密原因），这是不公平的，因为他们当然不是向后看的人，但市场是不公平的。

我最近遇到的另一位“商业领袖”雇主告诉我，他的开发人员分为拥抱人工智能和不拥抱人工智能的人，他说他发现软件功能开发“太慢了！”，并表示如果不是就业法，他会解雇所有不选择使用人工智能的开发人员。 我猜他是在开玩笑，但在没有提示的情况下大声说出这句话很有趣。

近几个月来，我参加了几次商业领导力类型的聚会，似乎只是简单地假设每个人都在使用人工智能来处理几乎所有值得谈论的事情。 我不认为他们真的是这样，所以观看这个故事的发展很有趣。

> the open arms embrace of subscription development tools (services, really) which seek to offload the very act itself makes me wonder how and why so many people are eager to dive right in

Here's a reason not in your list.

Short version: A kind of peer pressure, but from above. In some circles I'm told a developer must have AI skills on their resume now, and those probably need to be with well known subscription services, or they substantially reduce their employment prospects.

Multiple people I know who are employers have recently, without prompting, told me they no longer hire developers who don't use AI in their workflow.

One of them told me all the employers they know think "seniors" fall into two camps, those who are embracing AI and therefore nimble and adaptive, and those who are avoiding it and therefore too backward-looking, stuck-in-their-ways to be a good hire for the future. So if they don't see signs of AI usage on a senior dev's resume now, that's an automatic discard. For devs I know laid off from an R&D company where AI was not permitted for development (for IP/confidentiality reasons), that's unfair as they were certainly not backward-looking people, but the market is not fair.

Another "business leader" employer I met recently told me his devs are divided into those who are embracing AI and those who aren't, said he finds software feature development "so slow!", and said if it wasn't for employment law he'd fire all his devs who aren't choosing to use AI. I assume he was joking, but it was interesting to hear it said out loud without prompting.

I've been to several business leadership type meetups in recent months, and it seems to be simply assumed that everyone is using AI for almost everything worth talking about. I don't think they really are, so it's interesting to watch that narrative playing out.

在 HN 中查看
michael_j_x 2026/04/08 00:00
我真的很喜欢编码。我使用 Python、Rust、Java 甚至一些 Scala 构建了许多个人和专业项目。不过最近我对克劳德·代码上瘾了，尤其是超能力技能。感觉就像我可以用我的思想来体现代码一样。当与 Claude 一起开发时，我遇到了设计困境、架构替代方案、澄清问题，以及真正让我思考问题的事情。然后，我选择一个解决方案，提出替代方案，进行讨论，然后显示代码。我开始意识到我喜欢解决问题，而不是实际编写代码。就像我几乎克隆了自己一样，我的克隆人正在处理这些项目并回来向我寻求指示。感觉很奇妙
I really enjoy coding. I've build a number of projects, personal and professional, with Python, Rust, Java and even some Scala in the mix. However, I've been addicted to Claude Code recently, especially with the superpowers skill. It feels like I can manifest code with my mind. When developing with Claude, I am presented with design dilemmas, architectural alternatives, clarification questions, things that really make me think about the problem. I then choose a solution, propose alternatives, discuss, and the code manifests. I came to realize that I enjoy the problem solving, not the actual act of writing the code. Like I have almost cloned my self, and my clones are working on the projects and coming back to me for instructions. It feels amazing
在 HN 中查看
throw4847285 2026/04/08 00:12
“上瘾”“超能力”“用我的思想体现”“感觉棒极了”

为什么听起来你像是吸毒了？我知道这听起来非常粗鲁，但我想不出该语言的任何其他合理比较。

当这类认可写得如此夸张，用同样的陈词滥调，并且完全关注它给你带来的感受而不是它的作用时，你很难认真对待它们。

"Addicted" "Superpowers" "manifest with my mind" "it feels amazing"

Why does it sound like you're on drugs? I know that sounds extremely rude, but I can't think of any other reasonable comparison for that language.

It's hard to take these kinds of endorsements seriously when they're written so hyperbolically, in terms of the same cliches, and focused on entirely on how it makes you feel rather than what it does.

cbg0 2026/04/08 00:30
读了一堆与 Claude Code 相关的帖子，有些人对速率限制和模型智能表示真正的不满，而另一些人则似乎非常沮丧，因为他们已经达到了五个小时的限制，所以无法得到解决，这确实令人担忧 LLM 对某些人来说有多容易上瘾。
Reading a bunch of posts related to Claude Code and some folks voice genuine upset about rate limits and model intelligence while others seem very upset they can't get their fix because they've reached the five hours limits is genuinely concerning to how addictive LLMs can be for some folks.
在 HN 中查看
throw4847285 2026/04/08 00:35
我认为社会方面的报道不足。我认为这甚至适用于使用克劳德代码的人，而不仅仅是那些将法学硕士视为治疗师的人。换句话说，我想知道其中有多少人无法打电话给他们的医生预约或打电话给餐厅订披萨。我是作为一个努力做这些事情的人这么说的。

人们声称 DoorDash 和其他类似的应用程序是为了提高效率，但我怀疑很大一部分也是为了消除人际交互。 LLM 是一样的。或者，实际上，创建一个足够令人满意的人类互动拟像。

I think the social aspect is underreported. I think this applies even for people using Claude Code and not just those treating an LLM as a therapist. In other words, I wonder how many of these people can't call their doctor to make an appointment or call a restaurant to order a pizza. And I say this as someone who struggles to do those things.

People claim that DoorDash and other similar apps are about efficiency, but I suspect a large portion is also a desire to remove human interaction. LLMs are the same. Or, in actuality, to create a simulacrum of human interaction that is satisfying enough.

在 HN 中查看
vidarh 2026/04/08 00:41
它反映了我们从中获得的价值，相对于我们切换到 API 定价时继续使用的成本。它 是 当你面临生产力大幅下降时，你真的会因为达到极限而感到沮丧。

想象一下，作为一名优步司机，突然不得不转乘人力车几个小时。

It's reflecting the value we get from it, relative to the cost of continuing if we switch to the API pricing. It is genuinely upsetting to hit the limits when you face a substantial drop in productivity.

Imagine being an Uber driver and suddenly have to switch to a rickshaw for several hours.

djmips 2026/04/08 00:26
该药物是llm编码。 我有点明白了，当我还是个孩子的时候，第一次拥有计算机时，我在学习汇编语言后也有同样的感觉。世界是你的牡蛎，你可以做任何想做的事情。这就是为什么我几乎醒着的每一个小时都在电脑上工作。这种感觉最终消失了，但我花了一些时间和克劳德一起处理积压的项目，感觉又回到了过去。
The drug is the llm coding. I kind of get it, when I was a kid and first got a computer I felt the same way after I learned assembly language. The world is your oyster and you can do what felt like anything. It was why I spent almost every waking hour working on my computer. That wore off eventually but I've spent some time on my backlog of projects with Claude and it feels bit like the old days again.

guzfip 2026/04/08 00:16
> 为什么听起来你像是在吸毒，特别是可卡因？

几年来，这基本上就是我对整个硅谷的看法。

他们因滥用许多精神兴奋剂而闻名。马克·安德森不久前发表的愚蠢“宣言”听起来更像是阿得拉制造的胡言乱语，而不是一个连贯的政治宣言。

> Why does it sound like you're on drugs, specifically cocaine?

This has basically been what all of Silicon Valley sounds like to me for a few years now.

在 HN 中查看
throw4847285 2026/04/08 00:24
如果我走进树林，服用大量药物，写下我自己的怪人宣言，我的中心想法就是多动症是了解整个硅谷的关键。一群具有刺激驱动大脑的人创造了技术，为自己和其他民众提供越来越多的刺激，设定了新的基线，并要求新技术在无限循环中提供更高水平的刺激，直到我们所有人都刺激到死亡。延迟满足是敌人。

这类似于我们在进化编程中发现的技巧，可以在没有营养的情况下直接提供大量风味，并且我们一直在研究更复杂的方法来提供社交刺激，而无需其他人类（这也是人工智能对许多人的主要吸引力之一）。

当然，这些都是怪人的胡言乱语，应该被忽略。

If I were to go off into the woods, take a lot of drugs, and write my own crank manifesto, the central conceit would be that ADHD is the key to understanding the entirety of Silicon Valley. A bunch of people with stimulus driven brains creating technologies that feed themselves and the rest of the populace more and more stimulation, setting a new baseline and requiring new technologies for higher levels of stimulation in an endless loop until we all stimulate ourselves to death. Delayed gratification is the enemy.

This is similar to how we have already found hacks in our evolutionary programming to directly deliver high amounts of flavor without nutrition, and we've been working on ever more complex means of delivering social stimulation without the need for other human (one of the key appeals of AI for many people, as well).

Of course these are all the ravings of a crank and should be ignored.

skydhash 2026/04/08 00:13
这就像说喜欢作曲，但不喜欢演奏音乐。或者创造故事，但不喜欢写作。是的，它们是不同的活动，但又联系在一起。前者是创造力，后者是传播媒介。

代码是符号，就像乐谱或食物食谱一样。如果您与其他人的交互仅涉及最终结果（软件），那么。代码并不重要。但对于协作来说，确实如此。写得不好，只会增加大家的负担。

这就像强迫每个人用唱片而不是乐谱来学习交响乐。而且往往是录制得很糟糕的版本。

That’s like saying enjoying composing music, but not enjoying playing music. Or creating stories, but don’t like writing. Yes they’re different activities, but linked together. The former is creativity, the latter is a medium of transmission.

Code is notation, just like music sheets, or food recipes. If your interaction with anyone else is with the end result only (the software), the. The code does not matter. But for collaboration, it does. When it’s badly written, that just increase everyone burden.

It’s like forcing everyone to learn a symphony with the record instead of the sheets. And often a badly recorded version.

在 HN 中查看
vidarh 2026/04/08 00:46
> 这就像说喜欢作曲，但不喜欢演奏音乐

你认为这是不可能的吗？有很多人喜欢在追踪器等设备上创作音乐，但无意在乐器上演奏上述音乐。

我喜欢编码，但我也喜欢制作东西，两者是冲突的：当我为了写代码而写代码时，我一丝不苟，追求完美。当我制作东西时，我希望尽快行动，因为最终产品才是最重要的。

您所写的内容中还存在一个隐藏的假设：1）代码将写得很糟糕。有时是这样，但人们确实会这样做，但通常它比我制作的东西更好（例如，当需要用我不太熟悉的语言制作一些东西时），2）并且协作将与手动编写代码的人进行。但事实往往并非如此。

> That’s like saying enjoying composing music, but not enjoying playing music

Do you think that is impossible? There are plenty of people who enjoy composing music on things like trackers, with no intent of ever playing said music on an instrument.

I love coding, but I also like making things, and the two are in conflict: When I write code for the sake of writing code, I am meticulous and look for perfection. When I make things, I want to move as fast as possible, because it is the end-product that matters.

There is also a hidden presumption in what you've written that 1) the code will be badly written. Sometimes it is, but that is the case for people to, but often it is better than what I would produce (say, when needing to produce something in a language I'm not familiar enough with), 2) and that the collaboration will be with people manually working on the code. That is increasingly often not true.

在 HN 中查看
skydhash 2026/04/08 02:52
> 当我为了写代码而写代码时，

我很难理解这种比较。代码就是符号，你不能为了写代码而写代码。你有一个问题，你指导计算机如何做。为了你的合作​​者和未来的你，你要注意如何写它。 IMO 没有真正的区别。

> 在你所写的内容中还有一个隐藏的假设：1）代码会写得很糟糕

计算机并不真正关心您使用的编程语言以及变量和其他标识符的名称。人们确实如此。你可以拥有正确的代码（反编译的程序集或缩小的 JavaScript），但没有人愿意就此进行协作。

代码通常是某些过程的最精确的解释。通过正式，它是过程的真实表述。规格和文档可以描述事实，但不能体现事实。

您始终可以与 Markdown 文件协作。但最终必须有人查看代码并理解它的作用，因为这才是重要的事实。其他的一切都是祈祷和希望。如果您从不关心代码的可维护性和质量，这可能会是一个艰巨的过程。

> When I write code for the sake of writing code,

I struggle to understand that comparison. Code is notation, you can’t write code for the sake of writing code. You have a problem and you instruct the computer how to do it. And for the sake of your collaborator and your futher self, you take care of how you write that. There’s no real distinction IMO.

> There is also a hidden presumption in what you've written that 1) the code will be badly written

The computers does not really care about what programming language you’re using and the name of your variables and other indentifiers. People do. You can have correct code (decompiled assembly or minified JavaScript) and no one will wants to collaborate on that.

Code is often the most precise explanation of some process. By being formal, it’s a truthful representation of the process. Specs and documentations can describe truth, but they do not embody it.

You can always collaborate with markdown files. But eventually someone will have to look at the code and understand what it does, because that’s the truth that matters. Anything else is prayers and hope. And if you’ve never cared about maintainability and quality of the code, it will probably be an arduous process.

在 HN 中查看
michael_j_x 2026/04/08 00:18
用你的比喻来说，我喜欢作曲并喜欢演奏音乐。我不喜欢用笔在纸上写笔记的想法。我必须这样做，因为人们无法读懂我的想法，但如果他们可以的话，我会避免这样做。克劳德代码就是这样。编写的代码感觉就像我会编写的代码
Using your analogy, I enjoy composing music and enjoy playing music. I don't enjoy going through the notion of writing the notes on a piece of paper with the pen. I have to do it because people can't read my mind, but if they could I would avoid it. Claude code is like that. The code that gets written, feels like the code that I would have written

woctordho 2026/04/08 00:11
除了本地AI之外，一个严肃的选择是聚合API，例如new-api [0]。 API 提供商聚合了数千个帐户，其稳定性比单个帐户要好得多。由于订阅模式的工作原理，它也比官方 API 便宜，请参阅例如分析[1]。

[0] https://github.com/QuantumNous/new-api

[1] https://she-llac.com/claude-limits

Apart from local AI, a serious choice is aggregated API such as new-api [0]. An API provider aggregated thousands of accounts has much better stability than a single account. It's also cheaper than the official API because of how the subscription model works, see e.g. the analysis [1].

[0] https://github.com/QuantumNous/new-api

[1] https://she-llac.com/claude-limits

supriyo-biswas 2026/04/07 23:59

As an example, a long term goal at the employer I work for is exactly this: run LLMs locally. There's a big infrastructure backlog through, so it's waiting on those things, and hopefully we'll see good local models by then that can do what Claude Sonnet or GPT-5.3-Codex can do today.

I still use more traditional approach for finding bugs and other issues in my code, but the agentic workflow doesn't give me any net value.

在 HN 中查看
torben-friis 2026/04/08 00:48
人们总是会同意消除摩擦，即使这对他们有利。这是自然的偏见，我们倾向于不花费精力。

这就是为什么我们在外卖只需一个按钮的情况下就支付愚蠢的金额，这就是为什么我们接受网上约会带来的问题而不是在外面打破僵局，这就是为什么几十年来一直有骗局声称可以让你毫不费力地获得腹肌......

法学硕士是消除摩擦的终极方法。它们可以消除常规编程可以消除的间隙或机械工作，但更重要的是它们可以为您思考。

我确信这种人类模式与成瘾一样危险。但反对它要困难得多，因为谁会赞成付出更多努力而不是更少努力？资本主义的全部意义应该是它奖励效率。

People will always go along with a removal of friction even against their benefit. It's natural bias, we have a preference for not spending energy.

It's why we pay stupid amounts for takeout when it's a button away, it's why we accept the issues that come with online dating rather than breaking the ice outside, it's why there's been decades scams that claim to get you abs without effort...

LLMs are the ultimate friction removal. They can remove gaps or mechanical work that regular programming can, but more importantly they can think for you.

I'm convinced this human pattern is as dangerous as addiction. But it's so much harder to fight against, because who's going to be in favor of doing things with more effort rather than less? The whole point of capitalism is supposed to be that it rewards efficiency.

jimmaswell 2026/04/08 00:09
应急计划？像以前一样，不用它就可以编码。今天人工智能可能会消失，我会非常失望，但这并不是说我忘记了如何在没有它的情况下编码。如果说有什么不同的话，我认为它让我成为了一个更好的程序员，因为它消除了执行阶段的摩擦，并给了我更多的思维空间来进行抽象思考，而且这种好处肯定会延续到我的工作中，因为我们还没有批准副驾驶。
Contingency plan? Just code without it like before. AI could disappear today and I would be very disappointed but it's not like I forgot how to code without it. If anything, I think it's made me a better programmer by taking friction away from the execution phase and giving me more mental space to think in the abstract at times, and that benefit has certainly carried over to my work where we still don't have copilot approved yet.

stephbook 2026/04/08 00:06
我们可以在本地运行它，但重要的问题根本不会改变。

我们为晚上闲置的服务器付费，你找不到足够的系统管理员来解决当前的问题，开源模型不如闭源那么强大，提供上下文（如谷歌搜索）意味着你无论如何都可以将所有东西连接到互联网，你在哪里找到电源、冷却系统和空间，三年后你如何处理 GPU？

突然间，每个用户每月 500 美元似乎很划算。

We could run it locally, but the problems that matter simply don't change.

We're paying for servers that sit idle at night, you don't find enough sysadmins for the current problems, the open source models aren't as strong as closed source, providing context (as in googling) means you hook everything up to the internet anyway, where do you find the power and the cooling systems and the space, what do you do with the GPUs after 3 years?

Suddenly that $500/month/user seems like a steal.

cookiengineer 2026/04/08 00:35
最重要的是，您始终可以构建自己的自托管工具。没有什么是在家做不到的，只是计算一下你愿意花多少钱。

最近，RAM 危机仍在持续，使得此类事情变得更加不可行。但您仍然可以使用许多较小的模型来进行编码和测试任务。

规划任务 目前，我会使用云托管的任务，因为 gemma4 还没有出现，而且 GPU 价格仍然相当疯狂。

最酷和有趣的部分是，使用 ollama 和 vllm，您可以构建自己的代理环境 IDE，为其提供您喜欢的工具，并按照您喜欢的方式设置工作流程。而且这并不难做到，只是需要大量的调整和及时的摆弄。

最重要的是：使用 kiwix 自行托管 Wikipedia、stackoverflow 和 devdocs。为法学硕士提供一个使用搜索和阅读页面的工具，您的工作效率会迅速飙升。不再需要互联网，而且便宜的英特尔 NUC 已经足以自托管大量容器了。

来源：我正在为 Golang [1] 构建自己的离线代理环境，这是相当实验性的，但有时它也能工作。

[1] https://github.com/cookiengineer/exocomp

The great part is that you can always build your own selfhosted tools. There is nothing that can't be done at home, it's just a calculation of how much you're willing to spend.

Lately though the RAM crisis is continuing and making things like this more unfeasible. But you can still use a lot of smaller models for coding and testing tasks.

Planning tasks I'd use a cloud hosted one, for now, because gemma4 isn't there yet and because the GPU prices are still quite insane.

The cool and fun part is that with ollama and vllm you can just build your own agentic environment IDE, give it the tools you like, and make the workflow however you like. And it isn't even that hard to do, it just needs a lot of tweaking and prompt fiddling.

And on top of that: Use kiwix to selfhost Wikipedia, stackoverflow and devdocs. Give the LLM a tool to use the search and read the pages, and your productivity is skyrocketing pretty quickly. No need anymore to have internet, and a cheap Intel NUC is good enough for self-hosting a lot of containers already.

Source: I am building my own offline agentic environment for Golang [1] which is pretty experimental but sometimes it's also working.

[1] https://github.com/cookiengineer/exocomp

mvkel 2026/04/07 23:13
越来越多的证据表明，claude max 用户被放入一个大型计算燃料池中。随着 OpenAI 的国防部公关混乱，需求急剧增加（尽管 Anth 已经在与国防部合作？但我离题了……）。泳池达到了天花板。 Anth 没有剩余的计算可提供。因此，人们在 1 次查询后就耗尽了资源。 “努力解决它”意味着找到一种方法来提取克劳德代码，使质量下降不足以被注意到[0]，以便使计算池再次运行。蒸馏将继续进行，直到正常运行时间得到改善。

截至撰写本文时为 0，这一点很明显。很多“我应该继续吗？”并且“如果您想查看该信息，您应该运行此命令。”我一年没见过的路障+

Mounting evidence that claude max users are put into one big compute fuel pool. Demand increased dramatically with OpenAI's DoD PR snafu (even though Anth was already working with the DoD? But I digress...). The pool hit a ceiling. Anth has no compute left to give. Hence people maxing out after 1 query. "Working on it" means finding a way to distill Claude Code that isn't enough of a quality degradation to be noticed[0], in order to get the compute pool operational again. The distillation will continue until uptime improves.

0 as of this writing, it's noticeable. Lots of "should I continue?" And "you should run this command if you want to see that information." Roadblocks that I hadn't seen in a year+

在 HN 中查看
bmurphy1976 2026/04/08 00:53
还有另一块拼图。 达里奥（Dario）非常明确地表示，他们不会采取 OpenAI 的方法，即立即花费数万亿美元来扩大规模，并假设资金稍后会到来。 他们的支出大幅减少，并更快地实现盈利。

这意味着它们在基础设施方面将比某些竞争对手受到更多限制。我认为这是我们看到的一些限制。

There's another piece of the puzzle. Dario has very clearly stated they are not taking the OpenAI approach of spending $trillion to scale right now and assume the money comes later. They are spending significantly less and working towards profitability sooner.

That means they are going to be far more constrained infrastructurally than some of the competition. I think this is some of the constraints that we are seeing.

在 HN 中查看
mvkel 2026/04/08 01:12
他确实这么说过。并且几乎同时表示，如果他们希望保持 SOTA，他们将不得不花费数万亿美元，而他们必须做到 [0]、[1]。

他们没有计算能力，因为几年前他们没有玩游戏并获得优惠的价格，现在被迫与三流提供商合作。这不是一个策略。

我对他所说的一切持怀疑态度。

[0]“我们购买了很多。我们购买了很多。我们购买的数量与游戏中最大的玩家购买的数量相当。”

“在这个领域，盈利能力是一种奇怪的东西。我认为在这个领域，盈利能力实际上并不是衡量支出与投资业务的标准。”

[1] “你不能只服务于当前的模型，而从不训练另一个模型，因为那样你就没有任何需求，因为你会落后。”

所以他花钱不是为了让他们能够盈利，而且花钱和最大的玩家一样多，而且没有真正将利润视为衡量任何事物的标准？ K.

He did say that. And, in virtually the same breath, said they would have to spend $trillions if they hope to remain SOTA, which they have to be [0],[1].

They don't have compute because they didn't play the game and get the good rates a couple of years ago, and are now forced to work with third-rate providers. That's not a strategy.

I would take everything he says with a huge grain of salt.

[0] “We’re buying a lot. We’re buying a hell of a lot. We’re buying an amount that’s comparable to what the biggest players in the game are buying.”

“Profitability is this kind of weird thing in this field. I don’t think in this field profitability is actually a measure of spending down versus investing in the business.”

[1] “You don’t just serve the current models and never train another model, because then you don’t have any demand because you’ll fall behind.”

So he's not spending so they can be profitable, AND spending as much as the biggest players are spending, AND not really looking at profit as a measure of anything? K.

no grand strategy behind that

they're looking to IPO in 2028 vs 2030 for OpenAI, who have raised more than double the funds

so they're willing to play fast and loose with the terms and conditions of existing customers trying to make it happen

those pockets must be drying up really fast

在 HN 中查看
827a 2026/04/07 23:51
另外，我很惊讶没有人愿意谈论房间里的大象：氛围编码正在赶上他们。
Alternatively, the elephant in the room I'm surprised no one wants to talk about: the vibe coding is catching up with them.
在 HN 中查看
xmprt 2026/04/08 00:15
我认为没有人在谈论它，因为这不是一次富有成效的对话。我也不是特别看好氛围编码，但如果你能解释一下氛围编码究竟是什么导致了这些具体问题，那么讨论可能会更有趣。

但就目前情况而言，更可能的原因是芯片短缺和需求严重超过供应造成的产能紧缩。你的编码理由取决于他们的代码可能有多少共鸣。

I don't think anyone is talking about it because it's not a very productive conversation to have. I'm not particularly bullish on vibe coding either but if you could explain what exactly about vibe coding causes these specific issues then it could be more interesting to discuss.

But as it stands, the more likely reason is capacity crunch caused by a chips shortage and demand heavily outpacing supply. You vibe coding reason is based on as much vibes as their code probably is.

在 HN 中查看
leptons 2026/04/08 01:14
Vibe 编码通常不会产生高性能代码，它会产生意大利面条，其目的是让用户要求完成的工作尽快通过（通常勉强）工作的解决方案消失。

我最近将一个简单的项目从 Javascript 翻译成 C，其中 Javascript 产生 30fps，第一个 C 版本每 20 秒产生 1 帧。经过一段时间尝试让 AI 对其进行优化后，我从 C 项目中达到了 1fps。这不是一场胜利，但人工智能确实生成了可用的 C 代码。

我毫不怀疑，如果我自己做这件事（我很快就会做），并采取适当的护理措施，帧率将达到 30 fps 或更高。

Vibe coding does not usually produce performant code, it produces spaghetti with the goal of making the user asking for work to be done to go away as soon as possible with a (often barely) working solution.

I recently vibe-translated a simple project from Javascript to C, where Javascript was producing 30fps, and the first C version produced 1 frame every 20 seconds. After some time trying to get the AI to optimize it, I arrived at 1fps from the C project. Not a win, but the AI did produce working C code.

I have no doubt that if I had done this myself (which I will do soon), with the appropriate level of care, it would have been 30fps or more.

eatsyourtacos 2026/04/07 23:53

在 HN 中查看
kartoffelsaft 2026/04/08 00:03
问题是，当振动编码失败时（即它没有用，至少暂时没有用），通常可以通过更多振动来解决。再试一次，希望能成功。要求它进行重构，并希望更清晰的代码能对其有所帮助。如果您愿意自己考虑代码，您可能会问有关代码库的问题。高振动代码使用率既是它正在发挥作用的一个指标 和 它失败了。
The problem is that vibe-coding, when it fails (i.e. it's non-useful, at least for a bit), is usually solved by more vibes. Try again and hope it works. Ask it to refactor and hope the cleaner code helps it along. If you're willing to think about the code yourself you'll likely ask it questions about the codebase. High vibe-code usage is both a metric that it is working and that it's failing.

dyauspitr 2026/04/08 00:02
作为一个以前没有使用过 Claude 代码的人，我一直在使用 OpenAI 的 Codex，它非常神奇。我不知道克劳德到底有多神奇。

在 HN 中查看
Someone1234 2026/04/08 00:12
两者都很棒，不同之处在于：Claude Code 比 Codex 具有更好的直觉。这意味着它自然会产生像您（开发人员）一样的东西。

Codex 在我所说的“难题”上表现得非常出色。你把思维定得很高，然后让它发挥原始力量来解决问题。而 Claude Code 更适合日常“为我编写代码”任务。

所以差异有点微妙。您需要同时使用两者一段时间才能真正感受到它。

Both are great, where they differ is: Claude Code has a better instinct than Codex. Meaning it will naturally produce things like you, the developer, would have.

Codex shines really well at what I call "hard problems." You set thinking high, and you just let it throw raw power at the problem. Whereas, Claude Code is better at your average day-to-day "write me code" tasks.

So the difference is kind of nuanced. You kind of need to use both a while to get a real sense of it.

在 HN 中查看
mchusma 2026/04/08 00:23
我认为我和其他人使用它的方式是带有子句的代码、审查或使用 Codex 进行 bug 查找。然后我将评审结果传回克劳德以供实施。效果很好。在我看来，比法典实施更好，并发现与使用克劳德自我审查相比的差距。
I think the way I and others use it is code with clause, review or bug hunt with codex. Then I pass the review back to Claude for implementation. Works well. Better than codex implementation and finds gaps versus using Claude to review itself in my opinion.

在 HN 中查看
Someone1234 2026/04/08 00:06
Codex 刚刚改变了他们计算使用量的方式，带来了巨大的负面影响。

以前，订阅是获得 Codex 使用的最便宜的方式，但现在他们基本上具有 API 和订阅定价匹配（例如，$200 sub = $200 API Codex 使用）。

现在订阅的唯一价值是您“免费”获得 ChatGPT 的网络版本。就原始 Codex 使用量而言，您可以轻松购买 API 使用量。

编辑：目前已针对 Enterprise 推出，但很快就会针对 Pro/Plus 推出。下面说“我还没有遇到过这个问题”的人还没有*。

Codex just changed the way they calculate usage with a massive negative impact.

Before a Subscription was the cheapest way to gain Codex usage, but now they've essentially having API and Subscription pricing match (e.g. $200 sub = $200 in API Codex usage).

The only value of a subscription now is that you get the web version of ChatGPT "free." In terms of raw Codex usage, you could just as easily buy API usage.

edit: This is currently rolled out for Enterprise, but is coming to Pro/Plus soon. The people below saying "I haven't had this issue" haven't yet*.

第一天：2

第二天：3

第三天：1

Day 2: 3

Day 3: 1

aurareturn 2026/04/07 23:10
它们的 ARR 从 2025 年底的 90 亿美元增至今天的 300 亿美元。 3 个月内尺寸增加了 3 倍多。我期待着成长的烦恼。

就某些情况而言，仅 3 月份，他们的年收入就增加了 2 倍 Palantir、0.75 倍 Shopify 或 0.68 倍 Adob​​e。

They went from $9b ARR at the end of 2025 to $30b ARR today. That's more than 3x the size in 3 months. I expect growing pains.

For some context, they added 2x Palantir or .75x Shopify or .68x Adobe annual revenue in March alone.

aurareturn 2026/04/07 23:14
我认为他们的大部分中断都与这种疯狂的扩展和缺乏可用计算有关。

Vibe 编码并不自动意味着质量较低。自从我开始使用代理进行编码以来，我的代码库质量和整体应用程序体验都得到了改善。您可以利用人工智能来测试和编写新代码。

I assume most of their outages is related to this insane scaling and lack of available compute.

Vibe coding doesn't automatically mean lower quality. My codebase quality and overall app experience has improved since I started using agents to code. You can leverage AI to test as well as write new code.

在 HN 中查看
CharlieDigital 2026/04/07 23:23 

    > I assume most of their outages is related to this insane scaling and lack of available compute.
    > 
    > Vibe coding doesn't automatically mean lower quality
可扩展性是明智/实用的架构决策的一个因素。 可扩展性不会免费发生，也不会自然出现（事实恰恰相反），除非是明确设计的。 问题在于，将更多决策权交给代理意味着设计中的意向性较低，并且可能会导致扩展困难。

    > I assume most of their outages is related to this insane scaling and lack of available compute.
    > 
    > Vibe coding doesn't automatically mean lower quality
Scalability is a factor of smart/practical architectural decisions. Scalability doesn't happen for free and isn't emergent (the exact opposite is true) unless it is explicitly designed for. Problem is that ceding more of the decision making to the agent means that there's less intentionality in the design and likely a contributor to scaling pains.
在 HN 中查看
aurareturn 2026/04/07 23:31
我的理论是，他们的大部分中断都与计算和规模相关。 IE。一些 GPU 机架发生故障，一些客户发现错误。他们没有任何冗余计算作为备份，因为目前供应有限。他们愿意降低可靠性来最大化收入。
My theory is that most of their outages are compute and scale related. IE. A few GPU racks blows out and some customers see errors. They don't have any redundant compute as backup because supply is constrained right now. They're willing to lower reliability to maximize revenue.

bpodgursky 2026/04/07 23:48
这仅适用于可以在 AWS 中无限扩展而没有人注意到的小公司。

您正在谈论软件扩展模式，Anthropic 正在遇到硬件限制，因为它们正在最大化整个数据中心。 这不是一个架构决策，而是一场在需求之前提前运行数百亿容量的金融赌博。

This is only true for small companies that can infinitely scale within AWS without anyone noticing.

You are talking about software scaling patterns, Anthropic is running into hardware limitations because they are maxing out entire datacenters. That's not an architectural decision it's a financial gamble to front-run tens of billions in capacity ahead of demand.

    >  What exactly are emergent features when vibe coding?
Regression to the mean. See the other HN thread[0]

The LLM has no concept of "taste" on its own.

Scalability, in particular, is a problem that goes beyond the code itself and also includes decisions that happen outside of the codebase. Infrastructure and "platform" in particular has a big impact on how to scale an application and dataset.

在 HN 中查看
_fat_santa 2026/04/07 23:20
上周 CC 泄露后，我查看了他们的代码库，我最大的批评是他们似乎从不进行重构。

就我个人而言，我现在用代理编写了 80-90% 的代码，但在它们完成后，启动另一个代理来清理第一个代理编写的代码至关重要。

看看他们的代码，很明显他们没有这样做（或者做得足够）。就像主文件有 4000 个 LOC 一样，其中 10 个不同的功能都挤在同一个文件中。这种模式在代码中随处可见。

After the CC leak last week I took a look at their codebase and my biggest criticism is they seem to never do refactoring passes.

Personally I write something like 80-90% of my code with agents now but after they finish up, it's critical that you spin up another agent to clean up the code that the first one wrote.

Looking at their code it's clear they do not do this (or do this enough). Like the main file being something like 4000 LOC with 10 different functions all jammed in the same file. And this sort of pattern is all over the place in the code.

sheepscreek 2026/04/07 23:43

I have a buddy who used to work at Shopify and was proud about having sprints dedicated to removing unused features. This is really underrated but is the only reliable way to prevent bloat. Oh and getting rid of bloat is way more satisfying!

nonameiguess 2026/04/08 00:32
需要明确的是，这个数字将 大概 最终结果相当准确，但在初创企业世界中，这些财务指标变得多么糟糕仍然是一件令人烦恼的事情。距离 2025 年底还有三个月。你可能会认为，在我们以任何有意义的统计有效性水平说他们拥有 300 亿美元的“年度经常性”收入之前，我们希望看到至少两年每年赚取 300 亿美元的收入。
To be clear, this number will probably end up being reasonably accurate, but it is a pet peeve nonetheless in the startup world how shitty these financial metrics have become. We're three months from the end of 2025. You'd think we'd want to see at least two years of $30 billion dollar revenue earned in each year before we say with any meaningful level of statistical validity that they have $30 billion in "annual recurring" revenue.

kristjansson 2026/04/07 23:39
没有人会喜欢这个答案，但有一个简单的解决方案：支付 API 代币并调整您对 CC 的使用，以便您所采取的行动值得代币成本。

用一分钱买一美元固然很棒，但出售它们的人最终会想要收取一美元......

No one is going to like this answer, but there’s a simple solution: pay for API tokens and adjust your use of CC so that the actions you have it take are worth the cost of the tokens.

It’s great to buy dollars for a penny, but the guy selling em is going to want to charge a dollar eventually…

在 HN 中查看
Goronmon 2026/04/08 00:01
...支付 API 代币并调整您对 CC 的使用，以便您采取的行动值得代币成本

您是否认为“提示 -> API 令牌使用”连接有足够的可见性和稳定性，可以对使用 API 最终可能产生的成本做出可靠的估计？

就我个人而言，感觉就像根据“数据使用量”为 Netflix 付费，而我却无法提前知道任何给定的剧集或电影最终会使用多少数据，因为 Netflix 会不断地动态更改质量/压缩/等。

...pay for API tokens and adjust your use of CC so that the actions you have it take are worth the cost of the tokens

Do you feel there is enough visibility and stability around the "Prompt -> API token usage" connection to make a reliable estimate as to what using the API may end up costing?

Personally, it feels like paying for Netflix based on "data usage" without having anyway for me to know ahead of time how much data any given episode or movie will end up using, because Netflix is constantly changing the quality/compression/etc on the fly.

在 HN 中查看
kristjansson 2026/04/08 00:16
时间是相对较好的支出指标。 还有更多事后诊断，例如计数和成本，它可以写入状态行。

我同意事前这很困难，他们可以从某种估计模式中受益。

也许我们可以给出任务的尺寸，比如 T 恤？或者一群克劳德可以花费前 1M 代币为预期任务分配点值？

Time is a relatively good proxy for spend. There are also more ex post diagnostics like count and cost it can write to the status line.

I agree that ex ante it’s tough, and they could benefit from some mode of estimation.

Perhaps we can give tasks sizes, like T shirts? Or a group of claudes can spend the first 1M tokens assigning point values to the prospective tasks?

https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47664442

This reads like even if you had a rough idea today about what usage might look like, a change deployed tomorrow could have a major impact on usage. And you wouldn't know it until after you were already using it.
